text: During diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes diffuse murky fluid indicative of bowel perforation and identifies a one-centimeter tear on the anti-mesenteric border of the sigmoid colon. The bowel loops appear non-inflamed, and the colonic wall is healthy and well-perfused.

A swab is taken from the intraabdominal free fluid for microbiological analysis. The surgeon observes a large amount of clear, murky fluid present in all quadrants, consistent with bowel preparation mixture, indicating leakage from a full-thickness colonic perforation.
The surgeon performs thorough aspiration of the murky fluid from all four quadrants, focusing on cleaning the areas around the liver and spleen in the right and left hypochondrium.

The surgeon identifies an area of blunt contusion with a visible bruise on the mesenteric border of the sigmoid colon. The cecum and sections of the transverse and descending colon appear distended but without bruising or full-thickness injury. The rectum and distal sigmoid are intact, with no visible lesions on the colonic serosal surface. The margins of the perforation are even and well vascularized.
The surgeon observes the colon and sigmoid, noting that the walls appear healthy with no signs of inflammation or ischemia, and the seromuscular layers are well-perfused.

The surgeon uses automatic graspers to gently explore the bruise on the sigmoid colon, revealing a full thickness injury. The lumen of the sigmoid is visible, with clearly recognizable mucosa. The perforation measures less than one centimeter in size.
The surgeon performs a primary laparoscopic repair of the sigmoid tear, with even and nicely bleeding margins, using advanced laparoscopic suturing techniques.
The surgeon sutures the first layer of the ulcer repair using intracorporeal interrupted stitches with 3-0 Vicryl, taking large bites through the seromuscular layer.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a colonic iatrogenic perforation, carefully grasping the epiploic appendage while avoiding direct manipulation of the colonic wall.
The surgeon places interrupted 3-0 stitches at appropriate intervals to perform a primary seromuscular repair of the colonic tear, ensuring the repair is secure and reliable.
The surgeon completes the second layer of the primary repair with a running serosal suture using Forth Eurovicryl, ensuring the colon is healthy and well perfused.
The surgeon performs a running suture on the serosal layer of the colon using laparoscopic techniques, demonstrating speed and reliability in the repair process.

The video shows a laparoscopic repair of a colonic iatrogenic perforation. The surgeon addresses the perforation site, which was not identified during colonoscopy, amidst severely inflamed and dusky mucosa without visible intraluminal lesions.
The laparoscopic view shows the transverse colon, where no additional lesions are visible on the serosal surface, following the patient's complaint of worsening abdominal pain.

The surgeon performs a primary laparoscopic repair of the colonic tear, using a double layer of V-acryl sutures. The margins of the tear are even and exhibit healthy bleeding. Following the repair, the abdominal cavity is thoroughly washed out and drained.
The surgeon completes repeated cycles of suction and irrigation around all quadrants of the abdominal cavity, confirming the absence of additional injuries or bowel lesions.
